title: Take Home Exercise 1
text:
The dataset being used is the Mendeley Data, a detailed dataset of motor insurance policies with coverage-specific financial information. It is a real-world motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset from a Spanish non-life insurance company. It is particularly valuable as it is an open insurance dataset with real commercial policy and claims data. This data is in spreadsheet format and contains anonymised microdata on automobile insurance policies and covers the main operations of the non-life portfolio from 2022 up till 2024. The database has 354140 records.








Overview of the Dataset


Aspect
Details




Dataset Type
Real-world insurance portfolio data


Industry
Motor / automobile insurance


Country
Spain


Company Type
Non-life insurer


Time Period
January 2022 – December 2024


Number of Records
~354,140 rows


Number of Variables
47 columns


Unit of Observation
Each row refers to 1 automobile insurance policy transaction(current state/record) for a specific policy in a given year


File Format
Spreadsheet / CSV


License
CC BY 4.0


Main Use Cases
Risk modelling, pricing, actuarial analytics, fraud analysis, retention analysis, claims modelling

title: Take Home Exercise 1
text:
Counts of each policy type

The plotThe code














policy_counts <- dataset |>
  dplyr::count(policy_type)

ggplot(policy_counts,
       aes(x = reorder(policy_type, n),

                   y = 0,
                   yend = n),
               colour = "purple",
               linewidth = 1) +

  geom_point(
    colour = "purple",

    y = "Count"

CC Policy takes up majority of the transactions. TP makes up a small minority of the transactions.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
text:
Counts of vehicle brands

The plotThe code














brand_counts <- dataset %>%
  count(vehicle_brand, sort = TRUE)

ggplot(brand_counts,
       aes(x = reorder(vehicle_brand, n),
           y = n)) +

  geom_segment(aes(xend = vehicle_brand,
                   y = 0,
                   yend = n),
               colour = "purple",
               linewidth = 1) +

  geom_point(
    colour = "purple",
    size = 4
  ) +

  coord_flip() +

  labs(
    title = "Vehicle Brand Counts",
    x = "Vehicle Brand",
    y = "Count"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()




This shows the distribution and ranking of vehicle brands. Firstly, there is strong concentration among certain dominant brands. The top brands dominating the portfolio is “RENAULT”, “CITROEN”, “PEUGEOT”, “VOLKSWAGEN”, “FORD”, “SEAT” and “OPEL”. After these major brands, there is a sharp decline in frequency. This portfolio has a long-tail distribution where a few vehicle brands account for majority of policies while numerous other brands appear infrequently. There is portfolio concentration risk. If these dominant brands experience higher claim rates, are expensive to repair, become targets for theft then insurer exposure becomes concentrated. The concentration of policies among a limited number of vehicle brands may increase portfolio exposure to brand-specific behaviours and underwriting risks. Renault, Citroen and Peugeot are 3 major French automotive brands. The dominance of these 3 French brands likely reflect regional automobile market preferences in Spain, where European vehicle manufacturers hold substantial market share.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
section: Pareto analysis of driver risk groups
text:
Pareto analysis of driver risk groups

The plotThe code














library(dplyr)

risk_counts <- dataset |>
  count(driver_risk_group) |>
  arrange(desc(n)) |>
  mutate(
    cum_pct = cumsum(n) / sum(n)
  )

ggplot(risk_counts,
       aes(x = reorder(driver_risk_group, -n),
           y = n)) +
  
  geom_col(fill = "mediumpurple") +
  
  geom_line(
    aes(y = cum_pct * max(n),
        group = 1),
    colour = "red"
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    aes(y = cum_pct * max(n)),
    colour = "red"
  ) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(
    sec.axis = sec_axis(
      ~ . / max(risk_counts$n),
      labels = scales::percent
    )
  ) +
  
  coord_flip() +
  
  labs(
    title = "Pareto Analysis of Driver Risk Groups",
    x = "Risk Group",
    y = "Count"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal()




The red line in the Pareto Chart represents the cumulative percentage distribution. It shows the running cumulative proportion of the portfolio contributed by each driver risk group. Based on the chart, we can infer that the portfolio is heavily concentrated in middle-aged drivers. The senior drivers form a much smaller segment and young drivers are very minimally represented.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
section: Proportion of claim severity among claim records
text:
Proportion of claim severity among claim records

The plotThe code














library(tidyverse)
library(scales)

claim_severity_counts <- dataset %>%
  
  # Remove records with no claims
  filter(claim_severity != "No Claim") %>%
  
  # Count each severity category
  count(claim_severity) %>%
  
  # Calculate proportions
  mutate(
    proportion = n / sum(n),
    
    # Arrange severity levels in order
    claim_severity = factor(
      claim_severity,
      levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High")
    )
  )

ggplot(claim_severity_counts,
       aes(x = claim_severity,
           y = proportion,
           fill = claim_severity)) +
  
  # Create bars
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  
  # Add percentage labels
  geom_text(
    aes(
      label = percent(proportion, accuracy = 0.1)
    ),
    vjust = -0.5,
    size = 5
  ) +
  
  # Convert y-axis to percentages
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = percent_format(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Proportion of Claim Severity Among Claim Records",
    x = "Claim Severity",
    y = "Proportion"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
  
  theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    plot.title = element_text(
      face = "bold",
      size = 14
    )
  )




After filtering out records where claims have been made, half of the claims made are of low severity. The next largest proportion are of medium severity. The high severity make up the smallest proportion of all claims. This pattern is common in insurance portfolios.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
section: Trend of total claims over time
text:
Trend of total claims over time

The plotThe code














library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

yearly_claims <- dataset %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(
    total_claims_year = sum(total_claims)
  )

label_points <- yearly_claims %>%
  slice(c(1, n()))

ggplot(yearly_claims,
       aes(x = year,
           y = total_claims_year)) +
  
  geom_line(
    colour = "purple",
    linewidth = 1.5
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    colour = "purple",
    size = 4
  ) +
  
  geom_text(
    data = label_points,
    aes(label = total_claims_year),
    vjust = -1,
    colour = "black",
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 4
  ) +
  
   scale_y_continuous(
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0.05, 0.12))
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Trend of Total Claims Over Time",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Claims"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal()




There is an increasing trend from 2022 to 2024, reaching a total of 38937 total claims in 2024.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
section: Trend of all claims by Claim type
text:
Trend of all claims by Claim type

The plotThe code














library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)

claims_long <- dataset %>%
  select(
    year,
    liability_claims,
    liability_property_claims,
    liability_injury_claims,
    property_claims,
    theft_claims,
    fire_claims,
    glass_claims,
    legal_protection_claims,
    occupants_claims
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -year,
    names_to = "claim_type",
    values_to = "claims"
  ) %>%
  group_by(year, claim_type) %>%
  summarise(
    total_claims_year = sum(claims, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot(claims_long,
       aes(x = year,
           y = total_claims_year,
           group = claim_type)) +
  
  geom_line(
    colour = "purple",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  
  geom_point(
    colour = "purple",
    size = 2.5
  ) +
  
  facet_wrap(
    ~ claim_type,
    scales = "free_y"
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Trend of Claims Over Time by Claim Type",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Total Claims"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal()




This is a concise view of the trends for all the different claim types from 2022 to 2024.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
section: Distribution of loss ratio by policy type
text:
Distribution of loss ratio by policy type

The plotThe code














ggplot(dataset,
       aes(x = policy_type,
           y = loss_ratio)) +

  geom_boxplot()




In the plot above, we can see how loss ratio varies across policy type. From this plot, we can tell that most boxplots are compressed near 0, while there are many extreme points extending upward.The actual box and whiskers become tiny and flattened near 0, making them nearly invisible. This indicates that most policies have low loss ratios and a small number of policies generate very high losses. The distribution of loss ratio is highly right-skewed across all policy types, indicating that most policies are profitable or low-loss, while a small number of policies incur extremely high claim costs. The dots above the whiskers are outliers. COMP_E has the most extreme outlier, with CC and TPG having extreme outliers too. A key limitation of the current plot is that extreme outliers compress the rest of the distribution.
A zoomed in version of the plot:

The plotThe code














ggplot(dataset,
       aes(x = policy_type,
           y = loss_ratio)) +

  geom_boxplot() +

  scale_y_log10()




Differences in median is not extremely large. TP appears to have slightly higher median loss ratio.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
text:
Profitability by driver risk group

The plotThe code














ggplot(dataset,
       aes(x = driver_risk_group,
           y = profitability)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.alpha = 0.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Profitability by Driver Risk Group",
    x = "Driver Risk Group",
    y = "Profitability"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




The above plot is a boxplot comparing the distribution of profitability across different age groups. The profitability values are heavily negative. What can be inferred, is that most profitability values are near 0.There are also many extreme negative values, especially for the senior and middle aged groups. Senior drivers appear to be volatile, which could indicate that senior drivers have larger severe-loss events compared to young drivers.
Zooming in:

The plotThe code














ggplot(dataset,
       aes(x = driver_risk_group,
           y = profitability)) +

  geom_boxplot(
    outlier.alpha = 0.2
  ) +

  coord_flip() +

  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-5000, 2000)) +

  labs(
    title = "Profitability by Driver Risk Group",
    x = "Driver Risk Group",
    y = "Profitability"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()




The median for Young drivers is higher than the middle aged and senior aged drivers. The young age drivers group has much wider Interquartile Range. As there are large range of values ranging downwards and much fewer positive extremes, this shows that the large losses are much more extreme than large profits. Since the boxes themselves are above 0 for all age groups, this shows that mostly, insurers are profitable.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
section: Relationship between total premium and loss ratio
text:
Relationship between total premium and loss ratio

The plotThe code














ggplot(dataset,
       aes(x = total_premium,
           y = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm",
              se = TRUE,
              linewidth = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Total Premium and Loss Ratio",
    x = "Total Premium",
    y = "Loss Ratio"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()




This plot suggests that there is little to no strong linear relationship between total_premium and loss_ratio. Most points are concentrated towards the bottom, with only a few points having loss ratio above 9000 when the total premium is small. At low premium values, loss ratios are spread very widely, with loss ratio values ranging from 0 to 3000, and some extreme values above 9000. At higher total premium values, the loss ratio is concentrated around 0. This means that high premium policies are more stable whiile low premium prices are more volatile.
As outliers are compressing the graph, we will zoom in to see the trend more closely.

The plotThe code














ggplot(dataset,
       aes(x = total_premium,
           y = loss_ratio)) +
  
  geom_point(
    alpha = 0.2,
    colour = "black"
  ) +
  
  geom_smooth(
    method = "loess",
    se = TRUE,
    colour = "blue",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  
  coord_cartesian(
    xlim = c(0, 2000),
    ylim = c(0, 200)
  ) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Total Premium and Loss Ratio",
    x = "Total Premium",
    y = "Loss Ratio"
  ) +
  
  theme_minimal()

This scatter plot shows the relationship between driver age and total incurred claims. As points are compressed toward the bottom, most drivers have little to no total incurred claims regardless of age. There are several extreme outliers which represent rare but extremely costly claims. This shows that the insurance losses are driven by small number of severe cases. A small minority of customers can contribute to a large proportion of total payouts by insurer. Generally, the large extreme cases which lead to high incurred values are from drivers aged 35 to 80 years old. Very young (below 20 years old) and very old(above 80 years old) drivers show much less observations. The LOESS trend line suggests that there is no strong linear relationship between driver age and total incurred claims.

title: Take Home Exercise 1
section: Claim frequency distribution by circulation area
text:
Claim frequency distribution by circulation area

The plotThe code














library(ggplot2)

ggplot(dataset,
       aes(x = circulation_area,
           y = claim_frequency,
           fill = circulation_area)) +

  geom_violin(
    alpha = 0.6,
    trim = TRUE
  ) +

  geom_boxplot(
    width = 0.12,
    outlier.shape = NA,
    alpha = 0.5
  ) +

  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 2)) +

  labs(
    title = "Claim Frequency Distribution by Circulation Area",
    x = "Circulation Area",
    y = "Claim Frequency"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()




This plot shows a violin plot with a boxplot embedded into it. For a violin plot, the width of the violin represents where observations are more concentrated. Most observations are concentrated near 0, meaning most policyholders have very low claim frequency. This is typical and common insurance pattern. As the claim frequency is highly concentrated near 0 for both rural and urban circulation areas, it suggests that people make few claims despite the driving environment. The urban circulation plot appears slightly more spread out while the rural circulation is more concentrated at very low frequencies. The difference in urban driving environments could play a role in this. However, there could be other differentiating factors which are much stronger in explaining claim frequency.

title: MC2 Data description
text:
This folder contains the data for the anomalous postings originating from Tenant Thread.
This data is based on the actions taken by the employees and personal agents inside of Tenant Thread.


The anomalous posting is sent to SaidIt by John Windward on May 17, 2046 at 4:21am.



MC2_Data.json – A timeline of events gathered by the internal systems. org_chart.json – The organization chart of Tenant Thread including people and departments. Compatible with networkX node_link_graph.



Derived from data:
{
   "description": "string",
   "events": [
      {
         "short_name": "string",
         "parties": "array",
         "when": "float",
         "details": "object",
         "id": "int"
      }
   ]
}



The data is structured as a timeline. The events are ordered and indicate the operations performed by people, autonomous agents and office systems.
Field meanings: - short_name: The action taken - parties: Which people/agents/systems were involved. - when: Unix timestamp. The challenge occurs in May 2046, and timestamps should be in that month. - details: Additional information about the action taken. The contents vary by the contents of short_name. - id: A unique id for each record.

title: Hands-on_Ex05_treemap
text: This is about treemaps. Both treemaps and heatmaps use colour to represent data, but they are designed for very different purposes. A heatmap displays values in a matrix/grid format using colours. It is best for multivariate analysis and pattern detection. Treemap displays hierarchical data using nested rectangles. Rectangle size represents quantity, the size of rectangle varies. Rectangle colour often represents another variable. It is best for comparing relative sizes within categories.

title: Hands-on_Ex05_Building_Ternary_PlotWR
text: A ternary plot is a special type of graph used to visualise compositional data made up of 3 parts that together sum to a constant.This constant is usually 1 or 100%. Some examples include population groups (young, economically active, elderly), soil composition(sand, clay, silt) and budget allocation( savings, expenses, investments). Each corner of the triangle represents 100% of 1 component and 0% of the other 2 components.

title: Hands-on_Ex05_Building_Ternary_PlotWR
section: Ternary plot with colour by planning area
text:
Ternary plot with colour by planning area

The plotThe code














library(ggtern)

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
Violin plot with boxplot overlay
Unlike error bars which only show interval estimates, violin plots reveal: skewness, multimodal distributions, clustering, spread uncertainty.

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = RACE,
           y = MATHS,
           fill = RACE)) +

  geom_violin(alpha = 0.6,
              trim = FALSE) +

  geom_boxplot(width = 0.15,
               outlier.color = "red",
               alpha = 0.7) +

  labs(
    title = "Distribution and uncertainty of Maths scores by race",
    subtitle = "Violin plot with boxplot overlay"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
Bootstrapped mean confidence intervals

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = RACE,
           y = MATHS,
           color = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
    geom = "pointrange"
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Bootstrapped confidence intervals of Maths scores",
    subtitle = "Mean with bootstrap confidence intervals"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()




In the plot above, it appears blank as the points/error bars are probably very small or hidden due to grouping/ aesthetic issues. Another improved code will be to split geom from pointrange into point and errorbar. This makes the interval and mean point much more visible.

The plotThe code














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = RACE,
           y = MATHS)) +
  stat_summary(
    fun.data = mean_cl_boot,
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    linewidth = 0.8
  ) +
  stat_summary(
    fun = mean,
    geom = "point",
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Bootstrapped mean confidence intervals",
    subtitle = "Mean Maths score with bootstrap confidence intervals",
    x = "Race",
    y = "Maths score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_Visual_Uncertainty
text:
Half eye plot (ggdist)
The code below creates a half-density + interval plot. It simultaneously shows full distribution, median, credible confidence intervals and uncertainty spread. Compared to traditional error bars, half-eye plots show the full uncertainty distribution, reveal skewness, show density concentration and communicate uncertainty more naturally.

The plotThe code














library(ggdist)

exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = MATHS,
             y = RACE,
             fill = RACE)) +

  stat_halfeye(
    .width = c(0.50, 0.95),
    point_interval = median_qi
  ) +

  labs(
    title = "Half-eye plot of Maths score uncertainty",
    subtitle = "Density + interval uncertainty visualisation"
  ) +

  theme_minimal()




The differences between the 3 alternatives would be: 1. Violin + boxplot is best for detecting clustering/skewness and its main focus is distribution shape + spread. 2. Bootstrapped CI is best for non-normal data and its main focus is robust interval estimation. 3. Half-eye plot is best for Bayesian style uncertainty visualisation and its main focus is density + uncertainty together.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2
text:
Getting started
There is a need to check if we have installed the relevant packages.

pacman::p_load(ggrepel, patchwork, 
               ggthemes, hrbrthemes, 
               tidyverse)

Import and read the data set, which is the Exam_data.csv file

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")



Making different plots using ggplot.
First plot is to compare the English and Maths scores for Primary 3.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS,
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








Second plot is to compare the Science and Maths scores for Primary 3.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=MATHS,
           y=SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








I am going to plot the same type of graph to compare race and Maths scores.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=RACE,
           y=SCIENCE)) +
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method=lm, size=0.5,
              vjust= -.5)+ 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100))+ 
  ggtitle("English scores versus Math scores for Primary 3")








This plot appears strange as RACE is a categorical data type. Hence, the few values of RACE are compressed to the far left, making it difficult to interpret.
A histogram or bar chart would be a better choice to plot a categorical and numerical into one plot.

ggplot(data=exam_data,
       aes(x=SCIENCE))+
  geom_histogram(bins=25,
                 boundary=100,
                 color="grey25",
                 fill="grey90")+
  ggtitle("Distribution of Science scores")+
  theme_economist()








This plot is much easier to interpret. It is important to take note of data types when deciding what plots,charts and graphs to use.
I will make another plot to see the count of students by race. However, the code below will give an error as geom_histogram() is for continuous numeric data, but RACE is a categorical/discrete variable.That is why ggplot says:stat_bin() requires a continuous x aesthetic.

#ggplot(data=exam_data,
#       aes(x=RACE))+
#  geom_histogram(bins=25,
#                 boundary=100,
#                 color="grey25",
#                fill="grey90")+
#  ggtitle("Distribution of Science scores")+
#  theme_economist()

Instead, we can use geom_bar().

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = RACE)) +
  geom_bar(color = "grey25",
           fill = "grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Race") +
  theme_economist()










Using ggrepel:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")










Working with ggtheme package:

ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_economist()









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()









ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,
              base_size = 15,
              grid = "Y")










Combining more than one graph into a single display:
Here we will assign the individual plots to a variable.

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
          aes(x=MATHS))+
      geom_histogram(bins=20,
                     boundary=100,
                     color="grey25",
                     fill="grey90")+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100))+
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths Score")


p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data,
             aes(x=ENGLISH))+
  geom_histogram(bins=20,
                 boundary=100,
                 color="blue2",
                 fill="white")+
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100))+
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")


p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, 
              size=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

Now, combine the 2 plots into one.

p1 + p2








Lets combine 3 into one.

p1+p2+p3








In the above plot, some portions of the title are cut out. So, we increase the width and height of the area to plot.

p1 + p2 + p3








There are other ways to display the charts into 1.
“/” operator to stack two ggplot2 graphs, “|” operator to place the plots beside each other, “()” operator the define the sequence of the plotting.

(p1 / p2) | p3








tagging/numbering:

((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')








Inserting the plot at specific locations/areas:

p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)









patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()










Alternatives

Label only selected points instead of every point


nrow(subset(exam_data, MATHS > 80 | ENGLISH > 80))

[1] 117

ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_label_repel(
    data = subset(exam_data, MATHS > 80 | ENGLISH > 80),
    aes(label = ID),
    fontface = "bold"
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for P3")









Use geom_text_repel()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = ID),
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for P3")









Map color to a grouping variable in the labelled scatterplot


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS, y = ENGLISH, colour = GENDER)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = lm, linewidth = 0.5, se = FALSE) +
  geom_text_repel(
    data = subset(exam_data, MATHS > 85 | ENGLISH > 85),
    aes(label = ID),
    show.legend = FALSE
  ) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 100), ylim = c(0, 100)) +
  ggtitle("English V Maths scores by Gender")









Using a different theme instead of ggthemes. Use theme_wsj()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 20,
    boundary = 100,
    color = "grey25",
    fill = "grey90"
  ) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_fivethirtyeight()









Use theme()


ggplot(data = exam_data,
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(
    bins = 20,
    boundary = 100,
    color = "grey25",
    fill = "grey90"
  ) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 16, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 13),
    axis.text = element_text(size = 11)
  )









Combine 2 plots with wrap_plots()


wrap_plots(p1, p2, ncol = 2)









Adding overall title and subtitle


(p1 / p2) | p3 +
  plot_annotation(
    title = "Exam Performance",
    subtitle = "Distribution of Maths & English scores with relationship plot",
    tag_levels = "A"
  )

title: Hands-on Exercise 3
text:
Displaying standard deviation

The plotThe code











mean_sd <- function(x) {
  m <- mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  s <- sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  data.frame(
    y = m,
    ymin = m - s,
    ymax = m + s
  )
}

tooltip <- function(y, ymin, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean_val <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sd_val <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean_val, "\nSD:", sd_val)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data = exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS,
        tooltip = after_stat(
          tooltip(y, ymin, ymax)
        )),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,
    fill = "lightblue"
  ) +

  stat_summary(
    aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_sd,                 
    geom = "errorbar",
    width = 0.2,
    size = 0.2
  )

girafe(
  ggobj = gg_point,
  width_svg = 8,

title: Hands-on_Ex04_FunnelPlots
text:
Funnel Plot with highlighted outliers
This classified each sub district as above expected, below expected and within expected range.

The plotThe code














df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  filter(Positive > 0, rate > 0)

overall_rate <- sum(df$Death) / sum(df$Positive)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    se = sqrt((overall_rate * (1 - overall_rate)) / Positive),
    lower_95 = overall_rate - 1.96 * se,
    upper_95 = overall_rate + 1.96 * se,
    status = case_when(
      rate > upper_95 ~ "Above expected",
      rate < lower_95 ~ "Below expected",
      TRUE ~ "Within expected range"
    )
  )

ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate, colour = status)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = overall_rate, linetype = "solid") +
  geom_line(aes(y = lower_95), colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(aes(y = upper_95), colour = "grey40", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Funnel Plot with Highlighted Outliers",
    subtitle = "Sub-districts outside the 95% control limits are highlighted",
    x = "Cumulative Positive Cases",
    y = "Fatality Rate",
    colour = "Status"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Hands-on_Ex04_FunnelPlots
text:
Interactive labelled funnel plot

The plotThe code











df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  filter(Positive > 0, rate > 0)

overall_rate <- sum(df$Death) / sum(df$Positive)

df <- df %>%
  mutate(
    se = sqrt((overall_rate * (1 - overall_rate)) / Positive),
    lower_95 = overall_rate - 1.96 * se,
    upper_95 = overall_rate + 1.96 * se,
    tooltip = paste(
      "Sub-district:", `Sub-district`,
      "<br>Positive cases:", Positive,
      "<br>Deaths:", Death,
      "<br>Fatality rate:", round(rate, 4)

title: Hands-on_Ex05_Heatmap_for_multivariate_data
text:
Heatmaps are data visualisation techniques that represent values using colours. Instead of displaying numbers directly, colours are used to show the magnitude or intensity of values within matrix or table.
Typically: 1. darker or stronger colours represent larger values 2. lighter or weaker colours represent smaller values
Heatmaps consist of rows, columns and cells. Heatmaps are useful for detecting patterns, identifying correlations, comparing multiple variables simultaneously or detecting clusters or groups. There are different types of heatmaps such as Correlation heatmaps, geographic heatmaps, activity heatmaps, matrix heatmaps.

title: Hands-on_Ex05_Heatmap_for_multivariate_data

Plot cluster heatmap

wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix)

normalise the matrix column-wise

                      cexCol = 0.8,
                      margins = c(10, 4))

title: Hands-on_Ex05_Heatmap_for_multivariate_data
text:
Scaling method
Scaling method is commonly used when variables are assumed to follow or approximately follow a normal distribution. The transformation is performed by subtracting the mean and then dividing by the standard deviation.

The plotThe code











heatmaply(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)])

title: Hands-on_Ex05_Heatmap_for_multivariate_data
text:
Normalising method
Normalisation is a data transformation technique used to rescale values into a common range, typically 0 to 1. It is especially useful when variables come from different measurement scales, may not follow normal distribution or contain different ranges of values. Unlike scaling, normalisation does not standardise based on mean and standard deviation. Instead, it rescales values relative to the minimum and maximum values observed in the dataset. It preserves distribution shape.

The plotThe code











heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))

title: Hands-on_Ex05_Heatmap_for_multivariate_data
text:
Percentising method
Percentising is a data transformation technique that converts values into their empirical percentiles. Instead of focusing on the raw numerical value, or the distance from the mean, percentising focuses on the relative position of a value within the dataset. Percentising is useful because variables become directly comparable, extreme scales are removed and interpretation becomes intuitive. Unlike scaling, no normal distribution assumption is needed. Unlike normalisation, it is less sensitive to extreme outliers.

The plotThe code











heatmaply(percentize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]))

          hclust_method = "ward.D")

title: Hands-on_Ex05_visual_multivariate
text:
A parallel coordinates plot is used to visualised many numerical variables at the same time. Instead of just 1 x-axis and 1 y-axis, it creates many vertical axes, one for each variable. In the world happiness dataset used in this example, the axes could be GDP, Corruption, Freedom etc and each country becomes one line that passes through all the axes.
Parallel coordinates plots help you compare multivariate patterns. Eg. which variables move together, whether clusters/groups exist, how differently countries behave from each other.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
text:
Univariate: Policy type counts

View full analysis in report

CC is the policy type with the largest number, at 200,000 policies. It exceeds all others. TP has the fewest with fewer than 10,000 records. TP provides basic coverage, however CC offers more coverage. Customers strongly prefer comprehensive coverage over basic plans.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
text:
Univariate: Vehicle brand counts



View full analysis in report

Few major brands make up the majority of the portfolio. Renault has the largest at approximately 34,000, followed by Citroen and Peugeot at 29,000 each. Most top brands are mass market European manufacturers. This insurer has customers who are mainly mainstream vehicle owners, rather than high end brands.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
text:
Univariate: Vehicle age percentage distribution

View full analysis in report

Old vehicles make up a large portion of this portfolio, 91% of all insured vehicles. This indicates that the insurer mainly covers older vehicles rather than newly purchased vehicles. Older vehicles have very unique traits and portray different risk characteristics.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
section: Bivariate: Trend of all claims by claim type
text:
Bivariate: Trend of all claims by claim type

View full analysis in report

Most claim categories increased from 2022 to 2024. Liability claims recorded the highest volume increase from 7000 to over 18000. Liability property claims also grew sharply, increasing from around 6000 to more than 16000 claims. Property claims increased from about 3000 in 2022 to over 12000 in 2024. Occupant claims remained low with slight dip to 12 in 2023. Overall, the upward trend suggests increasing insurer exposure and possibly higher overall claims costs over time.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
section: Bivariate: Distribution of loss ratio by policy type
text:
Bivariate: Distribution of loss ratio by policy type

View full analysis in report

Loss ratios are highly skewed across all policy types, with most observations concentrated near 0. There are some outliers. COMP-E has the most extreme outlier with loss ratio exceeding 10,000 far higher than the rest. CC and TPG also contain outliers, with some exceeding 2000. While most policies remain profitable or low-loss , a small number of claims generate extremely large losses. High loss events are rare but have significant impact.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
section: Bivariate: Heatmap of circulation area by municipality type
text:
Bivariate: Heatmap of circulation area by municipality type

View full analysis in report

Type I municipality has the highest concentration of policies in both rural and urban areas, with roughly 115,000 rural and 112,000 in urban areas. Municipality type I dominates the portfolio. Municipality type C has more records in urban at 66,000 compared to rural which has only 34,000 records. Overall IS municaplity type has lower counts overall.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
text:
Bivariate: Claim frequency distribution

View full analysis in report

The distribution for both circulation areas are very similar. For both, most policies are concentrated near very low claim frequency values. The right skewed distribution shows that most policyholders make few to 0 claims. This indicates that circulation area alone may not be sufficient in explaining the differences in claim frequency. Other factors may have greater influence on claims behaviour.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
section: Bivariate: Profitability by driver risk group
text:
Bivariate: Profitability by driver risk group

View full analysis in report

Young drivers show the highest median profitability compared to other groups. It has larger interquartile range, indicating greater variability in outcomes. Middle-aged and senior drivers have similar median profitability levels, but both groups contain many extreme negative outliers, with losses extending below -5,000. Young drivers also have negative outliers, though fewer observations reach the most severe losses. Though all groups have extreme outliers, most policies still remain profitable, as the median is above 0. Driver risk group affects profitability distribution, young drivers appear more profitable.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
section: Bivariate: Relationship between total premium and loss ratio
text:
Bivariate: Relationship between total premium and loss ratio

View full analysis in report

The scatterplot shows an inverse relationship between total premium and loss ratio. Policies with lower premiums, below 500, display the highest variability and contain many extreme loss ratios exceeding 200. As premiums increase beyond 1000, the loss ratio are nearer to 0, with significantly fewer extreme outliers. This shows that lower premium policies are more volatile and prone to huge loss, while higher premium policies are more stable and predictable.

title: Take Home Exercise 1 Executive Summary
text:
Insights & Conclusion

portfolio is heavily concentrated in CC policies, old vehicles and European car brands.
claims activity increased from 2022 to 2024.
circulation area alone is not sufficient to predict claims behaviour
Most policies are low loss, small number of claims drive huge losses
lower premium policies are much more volatile

title: Take Home Exercise 2
text:
Event chain network

The plotThe code














incident_event_ids <- incident_window_events$id

incident_edges <- event_edges |>
  filter(event_id %in% incident_event_ids)

john_nodes <- c(
  "person:john_windward",
  "Agent/person:john_windward",
  "agent:person:john_windward"
)

incident_focus_edges <- incident_edges |>
  filter(
    str_detect(from, regex("john_windward|saidit|said_it|said it", ignore_case = TRUE)) |
      str_detect(to, regex("john_windward|saidit|said_it|said it", ignore_case = TRUE))
  )

incident_focus_nodes <- tibble(
  name = unique(c(incident_focus_edges$from, incident_focus_edges$to))
) |>
  left_join(event_nodes, by = "name") |>
  mutate(
    highlight = case_when(
      str_detect(name, regex("john_windward", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "John Windward",
      str_detect(name, regex("saidit|said_it|said it", ignore_case = TRUE)) ~ "SaidIT",
      TRUE ~ "Related Node"
    )
  )

incident_focus_graph <- tbl_graph(
  nodes = incident_focus_nodes,
  edges = incident_focus_edges,
  directed = TRUE
)

set.seed(123)

ggraph(incident_focus_graph, layout = "fr") +
  geom_edge_link(
    aes(label = edge_type),
    alpha = 0.45,
    colour = "grey60",
    arrow = arrow(length = unit(2, "mm")),
    end_cap = circle(2, "mm")
  ) +
  geom_node_point(
    aes(
      colour = highlight,
      shape = node_type
    ),
    size = 4
  ) +
  geom_node_text(
    aes(label = label),
    repel = TRUE,
    size = 3
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Focused Event Chain Network",
    subtitle = "Interactions connected to John Windward or SaidIT within the incident window",
    colour = "Node Role",
    shape = "Node Type"
  ) +
  theme_graph()

get_detail <- function(x, path) {
  out <- purrr::pluck(x, !!!path, .default = NA_character_)
  
  if (length(out) == 0 || is.null(out) || all(is.na(out))) {
    NA_character_
  } else {
    as.character(out[1])
  }
}

get_party <- function(x, position) {
  if (length(x) >= position) {
    as.character(x[position])
  } else {
    NA_character_
  }
}

get_contacts <- function(x) {
  contacts <- purrr::pluck(x, "contacts", .default = NA_character_)
  
  if (length(contacts) == 0 || all(is.na(contacts))) {
    NA_character_
  } else {
    paste(unlist(contacts), collapse = "; ")
  }
}

event_edges <- events |>
  mutate(
    from = case_when(
      short_name == "sent" ~ map_chr(details, ~ get_detail(.x, c("from"))),
      
      short_name == "propose_meeting" ~ map_chr(details, ~ get_detail(.x, c("a2a", "from"))),
      
      short_name == "queue_subordinate_task" ~ map_chr(parties, ~ get_party(.x, 1)),
      
      short_name %in% c("read_file", "list_files", "access_files") ~ map_chr(parties, ~ get_party(.x, 1)),
      
      short_name == "suggest_contacts" ~ map_chr(parties, ~ get_party(.x, 1)),
      
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    
    to = case_when(
      short_name == "sent" ~ map_chr(details, ~ get_detail(.x, c("to"))),
      
      short_name == "propose_meeting" ~ map_chr(details, ~ get_detail(.x, c("a2a", "to"))),
      
      short_name == "queue_subordinate_task" ~ map_chr(parties, ~ get_party(.x, 2)),
      
      short_name %in% c("read_file", "list_files") ~ map_chr(details, ~ get_detail(.x, c("target"))),
      
      short_name == "access_files" ~ map_chr(details, ~ get_detail(.x, c("file"))),
      
      short_name == "suggest_contacts" ~ map_chr(details, get_contacts),
      
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    ),
    
    edge_type = short_name
  ) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(from),
    !is.na(to),
    from != "",
    to != "",
    to != "NA"
  ) |>
  select(
    event_id = id,
    datetime,
    short_name,
    from,
    to,
    edge_type
  )

candidate_interventions <- centrality_nodes |>
  filter(
    node_type %in% c("Agent", "System", "File System", "File")
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(betweenness)) |>
  slice_head(n = 10) |>
  mutate(
    intervention_reason = case_when(
      node_type == "System" ~ "System-level control point",
      node_type == "Agent" ~ "Agent-level behaviour control point",
      node_type %in% c("File", "File System") ~ "Content-access control point",
      TRUE ~ "Potential control point"
    )
  )

candidate_interventions |>
  select(
    name,
    label,
    node_type,
    degree,
    in_degree,
    out_degree,
    betweenness,
    intervention_reason
  ) |>
  kable(caption = "Candidate intervention points ranked by centrality")

title: Hands-on_Ex07A
text:
This exercise is about learning to model, analyse and visualise network data using R.
Learning objectives:

title: Hands-on_Ex07A
section: Reviewing output of the graph object
text:
Reviewing output of the graph object
The output shows that GAStech_graph is an object representing a directed multigraph consisting of 54 nodes and 1372 edges. Each node represents an employee and contains 4 attributes: id, label, department and job title. The edge data captures interactions between employees, where the from and to columns indicate the source and destination nodes respectively. Each edge has a weekday attribute and weight attribute which represents the frequency or strength of interaction between the 2 employees that day. As the graph is a directed multigraph, multiple directed edges can exist between the same pair of employees, allowing interactions on different weekdays to be represented separately.

Visualising community

  mutate(community = as.factor(
    group_edge_betweenness(
      weights = Weight, 
      directed = TRUE))) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") + 
  geom_edge_link(
    aes(
      width=Weight), 
    alpha=0.2) +

    range = c(0.1, 5)) +

The plotThe code














g <- GAStech_graph %>%
  activate(nodes) %>%
  mutate(community = as.factor(
    group_optimal(weights = Weight)),
         betweenness_measure = centrality_betweenness()) %>%
  ggraph(layout = "fr") +
  geom_mark_hull(
    aes(x, y, 
        group = community, 
        fill = community),  
    alpha = 0.2,  
    expand = unit(0.3, "cm"),  # Expand
    radius = unit(0.3, "cm")  # Smoothness
  ) + 
  geom_edge_link(aes(width=Weight), 
                 alpha=0.2) +
  scale_edge_width(range = c(0.1, 5)) +
  geom_node_point(aes(fill = Department,
                      size = betweenness_measure),
                      color = "black",
                      shape = 21)
  
g + theme_graph()
